question: Jac's LLM integration is described as supporting any LiteLLM-compatible model. What does that imply about switching between cloud providers, and what option do you have if you want to run a model locally instead of calling a paid cloud API?
Why are walkers considered a natural representation of AI agents in Object-Spatial Programming?
In Object-Spatial Programming, what is the separation of concerns between nodes/edges on one side and walkers on the other, and what benefit does that separation bring?
In a Jac client-side component, what does declaring a `has` field correspond to in the compiled React output, and what happens when you reassign that field?
When writing package classifiers in jac.toml for publishing, why must the value be a TOML array rather than a plain string, and what goes wrong if you get it wrong?
In a fullstack Jac program, which parts of the code end up running in the browser and which on the server, and what in the source determines that split?
When client-side Jac code awaits a call to a function defined on the server, what is actually happening under the hood?
When a walker makes several report calls during a run, how should the caller read the results back safely, and why is hard-coding report indices without documentation considered fragile?
When you deploy a Jac application to Kubernetes using the built-in scaling support, what infrastructure gets provisioned for you automatically and how does outside traffic reach the application?
How does a walker decide which of its abilities to execute when it arrives at a node during traversal?

When values cross between Jac's client and server codespaces, what guarantee applies to primitive types like int or list[str], and what condition must non-primitive types satisfy to cross the boundary?
What is the difference between a walker ability declared with a bare `with entry` and one declared `with <NodeType> entry` — where does each one fire during a walker's run?
Inside a node ability in Jac, what does `visitor` refer to, and how does that differ from what `here` means inside a walker ability?
What's the difference between connecting two nodes with the plain `++>` operator and using a typed edge connection form, and when do you actually need typed edges?
How can a Jac edge-reference expression narrow a traversal by node type, by node attributes, and by edge attributes? Describe the general idea of each kind of filter.
Why would a walker restrict a visit statement to a particular edge type instead of just visiting all outgoing connections?
When a server-side walker reports typed objects rather than plain dicts, what does the calling client gain when it receives the results?
How does separating declarations from implementations change the way a large Jac package's folder structure communicates its architecture, compared to keeping everything in flat single files?
What does `disengage` do inside a walker ability, and how does it differ from simply letting the ability finish without calling visit?
What does declaring a walker with `walker:priv` give you in a multi-user Jac application?
When every ability in a walker calls `visit [-->]`, what overall traversal order over the graph results, and why does the visit mechanism produce that order?
Is production serving and Kubernetes deployment for Jac a separate package you have to install? Explain how the scaling capabilities and their heavier optional dependencies get into a project.
When exposing server logic in Jac, when is a `def:pub` function the right tool and when should you reach for a walker instead?
What problem does the `as` cast operator solve in Jac's type system, and when would you use it on the result of a generic function call?

How does a single Jac program replace the separate pieces of a traditional web stack — the database schema, the HTTP API layer, and React state/effect boilerplate? Name which Jac constructs take over each role.
A node declaration looks almost identical to an obj declaration in Jac. What does the runtime treat differently about a node?
If a `by llm()` call in Jac doesn't specify a model explicitly, where does the model configuration come from?
During a walker traversal, when do a node's exit abilities actually run relative to the visiting of that node's descendants, and in what order do the queued exit abilities execute?
What information does a walker's `path` attribute carry, and when is it populated?

Object-Spatial Programming is described as having 'bidirectional polymorphism' — both sides define the interaction. What does that mean for how node types and walker types each contribute to what happens during a visit?
How do you select an LLM provider in current mtllm/byllm code, and how does that differ from the older approach of separate per-provider classes like Gemini or OpenAI?
In a Jac test block, how do you verify that a function raises an expected exception, and why does the standard pattern place an `assert False` immediately after the call under test?

How does Jac's type checker treat imports from an installed third-party package that ships no type information (no py.typed marker and no stub files), and why does it not apply the strict Unknown discipline there?
When declaring a C function that takes a C string through Jac's native FFI, what type do you write in the Jac declaration, and why don't you spell out the low-level pointer type yourself?
After instantiating a node archetype in Jac, what step makes it actually part of a graph rather than a free-floating object?
Some older Jac material shows piping into spawn (forms like `spawn :>` or `spawn |>`) to pick a traversal order. What happens if you try that today, and what is the recommended way to spawn a walker?
In an AI-powered Jac app, why is it good practice to define one `obj` schema for the LLM's structured output and a separate `node` type for persistence, instead of using a single type for both roles?
Traditional OOP is said to 'bring data to computation.' How does Object-Spatial Programming flip that relationship?
In Jac edge-reference expressions, what's the difference between retrieving the connected nodes and retrieving the edge objects themselves, and when would you want the edge objects?

If a walker has cleanup logic that must run even when the walker might disengage partway through a traversal, why is putting that logic in an exit ability unreliable, and what is the recommended alternative?
What happens when you spawn a walker at root but none of its abilities ever call visit? Which of its abilities will fire?
What does building a Jac app for the desktop client target currently produce, and which parts of the desktop pathway are still work in progress?
What is the accumulator pattern in Jac walker design, and what problem does it solve when collecting data across a graph traversal?

When a Jac app is deployed with the scale option, how do environment-variable references in the configuration become available inside the Kubernetes pods, and what happens to that secret material when the deployment is destroyed?
What does byllm do automatically to reduce input-token costs when calling Claude models, and which parts of a request does it apply to?
When initializing mobile support for a Jac app, what tooling does the scaffold use under the hood, and how is the default target platform chosen if you don't specify one?
Why must the JWT signing secret be explicitly configured before taking a Jac scale deployment to production, rather than relying on the default?
What determines whether a node in a Jac program survives a process restart, and how much database setup does the developer have to do to get that persistence?
Besides appending a value to the walker's reports collection, what visible side effect does the `report` statement have, and why is that worth knowing when reading a walker's program output?
Why does the forgot-password flow in Jac's built-in auth endpoints return HTTP 200 even when the requested account doesn't exist?

When `visit` is invoked in the middle of a walker ability, does the walker move to the target nodes immediately? Explain the actual sequencing between the rest of the ability body and the traversal to the visited nodes.
